title: The first great tool: the database
content: In 1970, the relational database introduced a powerful idea: store data as tables of rows and columns, and query it with a language called SQL. This model was reliable, organized, and well suited to business records. It became a core foundation of business computing.
next: What were these databases actually built to do?

title: Built for transactions
content: Traditional databases were optimized for transactions: recording an order, updating a balance, booking a seat. This is called OLTP — many small, fast reads and writes that must be exactly correct. Getting a bank balance wrong is not an option.
next: So what happens when you want to analyze all those transactions?

title: Recording vs. analyzing
content: Running a giant report on the live transaction database slowed it down for everyone trying to place orders. Recording data and analyzing data pull a system in opposite directions. Companies needed a separate place built for heavy analysis.
next: What did they build to solve that?

title: Enter the data warehouse
content: The answer was the data warehouse: a separate system devoted to analysis, often called OLAP. Data was copied in from many sources, organized for reporting, and queried for business intelligence. For decades, warehouses powered the dashboards and reporting systems that businesses relied on.
next: What did you have to do before you could load data in?

title: Structure first, data later
content: Warehouses use schema-on-write: you define the exact columns and types before loading the data. This keeps data tidy and consistent, but it also makes change slower. If new data does not fit the predefined shape, it often cannot be loaded until someone redesigns the schema.
next: Why did that rigidity become a real problem?

title: The world stopped fitting in tables
content: The internet produced data that did not fit neat rows: web logs, free text, images, video, JSON from apps, and clickstreams. Much of the new data being created was semi-structured or unstructured, and warehouses were not designed to absorb that kind of input easily.
next: Couldn't they just buy a bigger machine?

title: Scaling up hits a wall
content: Traditional systems mostly scaled up: you replaced your server with a bigger, more powerful one. But bigger machines become expensive quickly and eventually hit practical limits. There is only so far a single computer can scale before cost and hardware constraints become serious problems.
next: Was the data at least easy to move and reuse?

title: Locked in closed formats
content: Warehouse data often lived in proprietary formats inside one vendor's system. That made it harder to access with other tools and expensive to move elsewhere. In practice, companies had less flexibility than they wanted over their own data.
next: What did this mean for the rising field of machine learning?

title: Machine learning was left out
content: Machine learning thrives on raw, detailed, flexible data. Warehouses offered the opposite: pre-summarized, rigidly structured data in closed formats. Data scientists were effectively locked out of the company's best-organized data.
next: So where did that leave companies?

title: Two worlds, no bridge
content: Companies were pushed into a split: warehouses for clean business reporting, and weaker solutions for everything else. Maintaining separate, disconnected systems was costly and slow. It also meant the same data was copied again and again, which increased the risk of drift and contradiction.
next: If the data won't fit the warehouse, what's the alternative?

title: A radical new question
content: Engineers asked a different question: instead of forcing all data into an expensive, rigid structure first, what if we stored it cheaply in raw form and worried about structure later? That idea helped launch the big data era.
next: That cheap, store-everything idea became known as 'big data'.
